segments.caption: The video opens with a black screen, then transitions to a bold red title card displaying 'FRONTLINE' over a cityscape, indicating the documentary's branding. The scene shifts to a protest in an urban setting, with individuals holding signs such as 'WALL ST. IS WAR ST.' and cameras capturing the event, suggesting public dissent against financial institutions. This is followed by a formal hearing or committee meeting, where men in suits sit at a long table with microphones and nameplates, indicating a governmental or regulatory context. The narrative then moves to a financial trading floor, showing traders intensely focused on multiple computer screens displaying stock data, with close-ups of hands typing and monitors flashing with market information. A woman in an orange top speaks directly to the camera, likely providing commentary or analysis. The video cuts to a bustling office environment with people walking through a modern building, then to a close-up of a 'WALL STREET' sign, reinforcing the financial theme. The sequence continues with a high-angle shot of a city skyline, followed by a train speeding past a platform marked 'To New York,' symbolizing movement toward the financial hub. The final frames show a train in motion, emphasizing transit and connectivity to New York City.

segments.caption: The video opens with a dynamic shot of the Train to New York speeding past a platform marked with a 'To New York' sign, establishing a sense of movement and purpose toward the city. The camera then shifts to an overhead view of the train tracks, capturing the train’s swift passage through an urban landscape. Inside the train, passengers are shown in a bustling, crowded environment—some reading newspapers, others engrossed in their phones, reflecting the daily commute of city dwellers. A close-up reveals a hand holding a newspaper with the headline 'MONEY & INVESTING,' hinting at financial themes. The perspective moves to the train window, showing the city skyline blurring past as the train accelerates, with silhouettes of passengers visible against the light. The scene transitions to a title card with bold red text: 'MONEY, POWER AND WALL STREET — A RISKY BUSINESS,' superimposed over the moving cityscape. This is followed by production credits, including 'PRODUCED BY MARTIN SMITH MARCELA GAVIRIA' and 'PRODUCER THOMAS JENNINGS CO-PRODUCERS LINDA HIRSCH BEN GOLD,' displayed over the same urban backdrop. The final shot shows a subway station with a 'Wall Street' sign as a train rushes past, reinforcing the financial district’s central role in the narrative. The lighting shifts from daylight to dusk, creating a moody, cinematic atmosphere that underscores the themes of power and risk.

segments.caption: The video opens with a dynamic shot of a subway train speeding past the 'Wall Street' station sign, establishing the financial district setting. Red production credits appear over the moving train, identifying the producers and writers. The scene transitions to a bustling street-level protest, where a diverse group of Protesters march with signs such as 'WALL ST. IS WAR ST.', 'FILTHY RICH', and 'EAT THE RICH', expressing anger toward Wall Street and corporate power. Police officers in uniform are seen managing the crowd, with a news van marked '4 HD' indicating media presence. The camera captures close-ups of individuals holding signs, including one reading 'TOO BIG TO FAIL HAS FAILED', underscoring the theme of financial crisis and public discontent. The protest moves toward a grand building, likely the New York Stock Exchange, with American flags visible. The scene cuts to an interview with David Wessel from The Wall Street Journal, identified by a lower-third graphic, providing expert commentary. The video then returns to the protest, showing a man in a black shirt raising a cardboard sign toward a crowd behind barricades, followed by a wide shot of demonstrators chanting and waving signs in front of the iconic building. The atmosphere is charged with energy, reflecting a moment of civic unrest and financial critique.

The scene transitions to an indoor interview setting where Dick Kovacevich, identified by a lower-third graphic as 'Chairman, Wells Fargo, 2005-09', speaks in a formal suit with a green patterned tie, seated in a softly lit room with classic furnishings. The camera zooms in on his face as he speaks, emphasizing his serious expression. segments.caption: The video opens with a haunting interior shot of a gutted room, its yellow walls damaged, debris scattered across the floor, and a broken window letting in daylight, suggesting abandonment or demolition. The scene transitions to an interview with Prof. Frank Alexander from Emory University Law School, who speaks in a warmly lit room, dressed in a dark suit and patterned tie, his expression serious and thoughtful. The narrative then shifts to exterior shots of dilapidated suburban homes—some with boarded-up windows, overgrown lawns, and peeling paint—emphasizing neglect and economic decline. One house, number 886, is shown in detail with wooden planks covering its windows and door, surrounded by tall grass. The camera pans across similar properties, reinforcing a pattern of abandonment. The segment concludes with an interview with Mtamanika Youngblood, an Atlanta housing activist, standing in front of one of the neglected homes, her curly hair framing her face as she speaks with concern, while the camera lingers on a cracked sidewalk overgrown with weeds, symbolizing decay and disrepair.

segments.caption: The video opens with a series of exterior shots of a dilapidated, abandoned brick house with overgrown weeds and boarded-up windows, emphasizing neglect and urban decay. A close-up reveals a padlocked door, reinforcing the sense of abandonment. The scene transitions to an interview with Roy Barnes, identified by a lower-third graphic as '(D) Governor of Georgia, 1999-03', who speaks with a serious expression in a well-lit indoor setting. The video cuts back to more shots of the abandoned house, then to a desolate suburban development with empty lots and unfinished homes under an overcast sky, suggesting economic downturn. Another interview segment with Roy Barnes follows, where he gestures while speaking. The video then shows the interior of a gutted house with yellow walls and debris, viewed through a broken window. The final scenes return to exterior shots of abandoned houses, including one with a porch and another with boarded windows, under a clear blue sky, before fading to black. The segment concludes with a FRONTLINE credit screen, thanking PBS viewers and displaying the PBS logo and Corporation for Public Broadcasting text.

segments.caption: The video segment begins with a series of funding acknowledgments, starting with the Corporation for Public Broadcasting (CPB) logo displayed in white text on a dark background, accompanied by the tagline 'A PRIVATE CORPORATION FUNDED BY THE AMERICAN PEOPLE.' This is followed by the MacArthur Foundation logo, presented in blue and green text on a dark, abstract background with faint digital imagery. Next, the Reva & David Logan Foundation logo appears in white text on a red background, followed by the Park Foundation logo in white text on a green background. The FRONTLINE Journalism Fund logo then appears in white text on a red background. The sequence transitions to the title card for 'FRONTLINE: Money, Power and Wall Street: Episode 1,' written and produced by Martin Smith & Marcela Gaviria, with the FRONTLINE logo in red at the top left. The credits continue to roll, listing key contributors such as correspondent Martin Smith, co-producers Linda Hirsch and Ben Gold, and editor Ben Gold, all displayed in white text against a dark, digitally textured background. Additional credits include field producer Sabrina Shankman, director of photography Timothy Grucza, and narrator William H. Macy. The credits also acknowledge archival materials from sources like ABCNEWS VideoSource, CNN ImageSource, Getty Images, and others. The segment concludes with a promotional graphic for the DVD of the series, priced at $29.99 plus S&H, available at shopPBS.org and on iTunes, alongside the final production credits including executive producers David Fanning and Raney Aronson-Rath. Throughout, the background remains dark with subtle digital effects, and a URL 'pbs.org/frontline' is consistently displayed on the right side of the screen.
